title: Apply: the moon and its motions
description: Apply your understanding of the moon and its motions in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS
Apply: the moon and its motions

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the moon orbiting Earth over the course of a month. Four different positions in the moon’s orbit are labeled A through D.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which position would the moon appear full to the people on Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the moon appears full when it is on the *opposite* side of Earth from the sun.
Looking at the diagram, we can see that the moon and the sun are on opposite sides of Earth when the moon is at position C.
So, the moon would appear full at position C.

options.alt: Earth is shown to the right of the sun. Earth is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half. The moon is shown orbiting Earth. At position A, the moon is between Earth and the sun. At position B, the moon is directly below Earth. At position C, the moon is to the right of Earth. At position D, the moon is directly above Earth. At each position, the moon is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the moon orbiting Earth over the course of a month. Four different positions in the moon’s orbit are labeled A through D.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which position would the moon appear new to the people on Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the moon appears new when it is on the *same* side of Earth as the sun.
Looking at the diagram, we can see that the moon and the sun are on the same side of Earth when the moon is at position A.
So, the moon would appear new at position A.

options.alt: Earth is shown to the right of the sun. Earth is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half. The moon is shown orbiting Earth. At position A, the moon is between Earth and the sun. At position B, the moon is directly below Earth. At position C, the moon is to the right of Earth. At position D, the moon is directly above Earth. At each position, the moon is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the moon orbiting Earth over the course of a month. Four different positions in the moon’s orbit are labeled A through D.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which two positions would the moon appear as a quarter moon to the people on Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the moon appears as a quarter moon when it forms a *right angle* with Earth and the sun.
Looking at the diagram, we can see that the moon, Earth, and the sun form a right angle when the moon is at either position B or position D.
So, the moon would appear as a quarter moon at positions B and D.

options.alt: Earth is shown to the right of the sun. Earth is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half. The moon is shown orbiting Earth. At position A, the moon is between Earth and the sun. At position B, the moon is directly below Earth. At position C, the moon is to the right of Earth. At position D, the moon is directly above Earth. At each position, the moon is sunlit on the left half and shaded on the right half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the phase of the moon as observed every seven days for a month.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which image shows the moon phase that was observed on Day $21$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s first consider the moon phases on the days surrounding Day $21$. On Day $14$, the moon phase was a full moon. On Day $28$, the moon phase was a new moon. Since Day $21$ occurred exactly halfway between these two days, the moon phase on Day $21$ must have been a *quarter* moon.

Now, let’s determine whether this quarter moon was lit on the right or left side. According to the diagram, the quarter moon on Day $7$ was lit on the *right* side. Since the two quarter moons in a moon cycle are always lit on opposite sides, the quarter moon on Day $21$ must have been lit on the *left* side.

So, the moon phase that was observed on Day $21$ was

![Half of the moon is sunlit on the left. The right half is shaded.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62bc865c9dd1785c9666b88084bf3235.svg)

options.alt: On Day 0, the moon is fully shaded. On Day 7, half of the moon is shaded on the left. The right half is sunlit. On Day 14, the moon is fully sunlit. On Day 21, a question mark is shown instead of a moon phase. On Day 28, the moon is fully shaded.

choices.content: ![Half of the moon is shaded on the left. The right half is sunlit.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/44795536949a6069d1b7150dceb41119.svg)
![Half of the moon is sunlit on the left. The right half is shaded.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62bc865c9dd1785c9666b88084bf3235.svg)
![Most of the moon is shaded on the left. A sliver on the right side is sunlit.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d1e8209c9601bfbce2f15baa0f7e7c79.svg)
![Most of the moon is shaded on the right. A sliver on the left side is sunlit.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9a71945d753e1c06e0e5540308dd064d.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the shadow cast by Earth at one point during the year. An observer is located on Earth at the point indicated.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the diagram, would the observer see a lunar eclipse at this point during the year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A lunar eclipse occurs when the moon is covered by Earth’s shadow. When the moon is partially covered, the eclipse is *partial*. When the moon is fully covered, the eclipse is *total*.
In this case, the diagram shows that the moon is *fully* covered by Earth’s shadow at this point during the year.
So, the observer would see a total lunar eclipse.

options.alt: Earth is shown to the right of the sun, and the moon is shown to the right of Earth. The moon is fully covered by Earth’s shadow. The observer is located on the dark side of Earth, farthest from the sun.

choices.content: Yes, the observer would see a partial lunar eclipse.
Yes, the observer would see a total lunar eclipse.
No, the observer would not see a lunar eclipse.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the shadow cast by Earth at one point during the year. An observer is located on Earth at the point indicated.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the diagram, would the observer see a lunar eclipse at this point during the year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A lunar eclipse occurs when the moon is covered by Earth’s shadow. When the moon is partially covered, the eclipse is *partial*. When the moon is fully covered, the eclipse is *total*.
In this case, the diagram shows that the moon is *partially* covered by Earth’s shadow at this point during the year.
So, the observer would see a partial lunar eclipse.

options.alt: Earth is shown to the right of the sun, and the moon is shown to the right and slightly down of Earth. Approximately half of the moon is covered by Earth’s shadow. The observer is located on the dark side of Earth, farthest from the sun.

choices.content: Yes, the observer would see a partial lunar eclipse.
Yes, the observer would see a total lunar eclipse.
No, the observer would not see a lunar eclipse.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amara recorded the dates of four major moon phases during the month of September. Her results are shown in the table below.

Moon phase | Date
:-: | :-:
third quarter | September 5
new moon | September 12
first quarter | September 21
full moon | September 27

**Using Amara’s data, predict the date on which the next new moon will occur.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the phases of the moon repeat about once a month.
This means that if Amara recorded a new moon on September 12, then the next new moon will occur approximately a month later.
So, the next new moon will occur around October 12.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chen recorded the dates of four major moon phases during the month of November. His results are shown in the table below.

Moon phase | Date
:-: | :-:
third quarter | November 8
new moon | November 14
first quarter | November 22
full moon | November 30

**Using Chen’s data, predict the date on which the next full moon will occur.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the phases of the moon repeat about once a month.
This means that if Chen recorded a full moon on November 30, then the next full moon will occur approximately a month later.
So, the next full moon will occur around December 30.

assessment_items.question_content:
Noemi recorded the dates of four major moon phases during the month of February. Her results are shown in the table below.

Moon phase | Date
:-: | :-:
full moon | February 6
third quarter | February 13
new moon | February 20
first quarter | February 27

**Using Noemi’s data, predict the date on which the next full moon will occur.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that the phases of the moon repeat about once a month.
This means that if Noemi recorded a full moon on February 6, then the next full moon will occur approximately a month later.
So, the next full moon will occur around March 6.
